title: How the body develops insomnia as a protective mechanism
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You're not broken. Your body is trying to keep you alive.

I know what it's like to lie awake at 3:00 AM, staring at the ceiling, your mind racing through a list of everything that's ever gone wrong. Your heart is pounding. Your jaw is clenched. You're exhausted, but sleep won't come. And then the shame spiral starts: "What's wrong with me? Why can't I just rest like a normal person?"

Here's the thing. You're not broken. You're not failing. And you're definitely not alone.

What if I told you that your insomnia isn't a flaw in your biology? What if it's actually a brilliant, if exhausting, protection strategy your body developed to keep you safe?

Stay with me here. Because this changes everything about how we approach sleep problems.

I spent years hating my own body for refusing to sleep. I tried every supplement, every meditation app, every blackout curtain. Nothing worked long term. And then I learned about the polyvagal theory from Stephen Porges, and suddenly my insomnia made terrifying, beautiful sense.

Your body's nervous system has one primary job: keep you alive. That's it. It doesn't care about your productivity tomorrow. It doesn't care about your social obligations. It cares about survival. And when your nervous system detects threat, it activates a cascade of physiological responses designed to keep you alert, vigilant, and ready to fight or flee.

Sleep is the opposite of vigilance. Sleep is vulnerability. Sleep is trust. And for a traumatized nervous system, trust is a luxury it simply cannot afford.

The night watchman inside your skull

Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. Your brain stores memories, sure. But your body stores the unfinished survival responses. The tensed muscles that never released. The held breath that never exhaled. The hypervigilance that never turned off.

And here's what most people don't get: your body doesn't know the difference between a real threat right now and a remembered threat from ten years ago. It doesn't know that the abuse ended, that the accident happened, that the war is over. All it knows is that something terrible happened, and it's determined not to let that happen again.

So it keeps you awake. It keeps you scanning. It keeps you ready.

Think about it. In the wild, if a predator attacked your tribe and you survived, would you sleep soundly that night? No. You'd stay awake, listening for the predator's return. You'd sleep in shifts. You'd startle at every sound. Your insomnia would be a feature, not a bug. It would be what kept you alive.

That's what's happening inside you. Your nervous system is running ancient software designed for saber-toothed tigers, but it's running it in response to childhood neglect, emotional abuse, or a car accident from 2015.

I'm not saying this to minimize your pain. I'm saying it because I need you to understand that your body is not your enemy. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's just working with outdated information.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk lays out in clear, compassionate language how trauma reshapes the brain and body, including sleep architecture. It's not light reading, but it's the kind of book that makes you feel less crazy.

How hyperarousal hijacks your sleep cycle

Your sleep-wake cycle is governed by your autonomic nervous system. There's the sympathetic branch, which is your gas pedal. It's responsible for arousal, alertness, and the fight-or-flight response. And there's the parasympathetic branch, which is your brake. It's responsible for rest, digestion, and sleep.

For a healthy nervous system, these two branches dance. They take turns. When it's time to sleep, the brake engages. Your heart rate slows. Your breathing deepens. Your muscles relax. You drift off.

But for a traumatized nervous system, the gas pedal is stuck. Partially, maybe, or fully. Your sympathetic nervous system is in a state of chronic hyperarousal. Your cortisol levels are elevated. Your adrenaline is pumping. Your body is saying, "Danger. Stay awake. Stay ready."

And here's the cruel irony: the more exhausted you become, the more your nervous system interprets that exhaustion as a threat. Because in survival mode, being too tired to respond to danger is itself dangerous. So your body pushes back harder. It keeps you wired even as you're crashing.

Wild, right? Your body is literally keeping you awake because it's afraid you'll be too tired to protect yourself from the thing that already happened.

This is why "sleep hygiene" advice can feel so insulting. Someone telling you to put your phone away and drink chamomile tea when your nervous system is screaming DANGER is like telling someone to relax while being chased by a bear. It's not that the advice is wrong. It's that it's addressing the wrong layer of the problem.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. It dives deep into how the body holds trauma and how we can gently, slowly help it release those patterns. It's not a quick fix. But it's honest about the process.

The freeze response and its twisted relationship with sleep

Not all trauma responses look like fight or flight. Many of us, especially those who experienced prolonged, inescapable trauma, default to the freeze response. This is the "play dead" option. It's what happens when fighting or fleeing aren't possible.

And here's where it gets tricky. Freeze can look a lot like sleep. You collapse. You go numb. You disconnect from your body. Your energy drains. You might sleep twelve, fourteen hours a day. But it's not restorative sleep. It's collapse. It's the nervous system hitting the emergency brake so hard that everything shuts down.

But even in freeze, the body isn't safe. It's still in survival mode. It's just using a different strategy. And when the freeze starts to thaw, which happens as you begin to heal, the hyperarousal often comes roaring back. That's when insomnia can hit hardest.

I've seen this pattern in myself and in countless others. You start therapy. You start feeling your feelings for the first time in years. And suddenly you can't sleep. You're waking up at 2:00 AM with your heart racing. You're having nightmares. You're exhausted but wired.

This is not a sign that healing is making things worse. It's a sign that your nervous system is starting to process what it's been holding. But it's also a sign that you need more support, more grounding, more gentle resourcing.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It explains the freeze response in detail and offers practical ways to help the body complete those unfinished survival responses. It's a classic for a reason.

What actually helps when your body won't let you sleep

I'm not going to give you a 10-step sleep protocol. Those never worked for me, and they probably haven't worked for you either. But I can share what I've learned about working with, not against, a protective nervous system.

First, stop fighting. The more you try to force sleep, the more your nervous system interprets your desperation as a threat. "She's panicking about sleep. Something must be wrong. Better stay awake to figure it out." I know this sounds backward, but the most helpful thing I ever did was stop trying to sleep.

Second, orient to safety. Stephen Porges talks about "neuroception," the subconscious scanning your nervous system does for cues of safety or danger. Your insomnia might be your nervous system saying it doesn't feel safe enough to sleep. So instead of trying to sleep, try to find small moments of safety. Feel your back against the bed. Notice the weight of the blanket. Listen for a distant sound that's predictable and non-threatening. A fan. Rain outside. Your own breathing.

Third, move the energy. Sometimes your body is holding so much tension that sleep is physically impossible. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it gently before bed, focusing on the back and hips, can help signal to your nervous system that it's safe to start letting go. But go slow. Your body needs to feel in control of the process.

Fourth, be honest about what your insomnia is protecting you from. For me, it was the fear of being vulnerable. Sleep meant letting my guard down completely, and my body had learned that letting my guard down meant getting hurt. Once I understood that, I could start having compassion for my insomnia instead of hating it.

Fifth, find a therapist who understands trauma and the body. Someone trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or Internal Family Systems (IFS) can help you work with your nervous system directly. Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation is also incredibly helpful for understanding why different parts of you might have different relationships with sleep.

And sixth, be patient. Your body developed this protection over years, possibly decades. It's not going to drop it overnight just because you understand it intellectually. The healing happens in the body, and the body has its own timeline.

I still have nights where sleep won't come. But I don't panic anymore. I don't hate myself. I get up, make some tea, sit in the dark, and let my body do what it needs to do. Sometimes I write. Sometimes I just breathe. I've learned that my insomnia is not a sign that I'm failing at healing. It's a sign that my body is still trying to protect me, and that deserves respect, not shame.

Your insomnia is not a curse. It's a message. And when you learn to listen to it, instead of fighting it, something shifts. Not overnight. Not perfectly. But slowly, gently, your body starts to learn that it's safe enough to rest.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's work on how trauma reshapes the brain and body
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of how the body holds and releases trauma
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic guide to healing trauma through the body
  • TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link) - A tool for gently releasing stored physical tension
